content:
# Genesis Chapter 43 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 25v–26r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_043_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_043_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 43–44 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0047

---

## The Famine Severe: Jacob Sends Benjamin

**1** And the famine was severe in the land.

**2** And it came to pass when they had finished eating the grain that they had brought from Egypt, that their father said to them, "Go back, buy us a little food."

**3** And Judah said to him, saying, "The man solemnly warned us, saying, 'You shall not see my face unless your brother is with you.'

**4** If you will send our brother with us, we will go down and buy food for you.

**5** But if you will not send him, we will not go down, for the man said to us, 'You shall not see my face unless your brother is with you.'"

**6** And Israel said, "Why did you do evil to me, to tell the man that you had another brother?"

**7** And they said, "The man asked carefully about us and about our kindred, saying, 'Is your father still alive? Do you have another brother?' And we told him according to these words. Could we know at all that he would say, 'Bring your brother down'?"

## Judah Stands as Surety for Benjamin

**8** And Judah said to Israel his father, "Send the boy with me, and we will arise and go, that we may live and not die — both we and you and also our little ones.

**9** I myself will be surety for him — from my hand you may require him. If I do not bring him back to you and set him before you, then I will have sinned against you all the days of my life.

**10** For if we had not delayed, surely by now we would have returned twice."

## Jacob Relents: The Gift and El Shaddai's Mercy

**11** And Israel their father said to them, "If it is so, then do this: take of the choicest products of the land in your vessels and bring down to the man a gift — a little balm and a little honey, tragacanth gum and laudanum, pistachios and almonds.

**12** And take double the silver in your hand, and the silver that was returned in the mouth of your sacks you shall bring back in your hand — perhaps it was an oversight.

**13** Take also your brother, and arise, go back to the man.

**14** And may El Shaddai give you compassion before the man, that he may release to you your other brother and Benjamin. And as for me — if I am bereaved, I am bereaved."

**15** And the men took this gift, and they took double the silver in their hand, and Benjamin. And they arose and went down to Egypt and stood before Joseph.

## Joseph Sees Benjamin: The Steward and the Fear

**16** And Joseph saw Benjamin with them, and he said to the one who was over his house, "Bring the men into the house, and slaughter an animal and prepare it, for the men will eat with me at noon."

**17** And the man did as Joseph said, and the man brought the men to Joseph's house.

**18** And the men were afraid because they were brought to Joseph's house, and they said, "Because of the silver that was returned in our sacks the first time we are being brought in, in order to roll over us and fall upon us and take us as slaves, and our donkeys."

**19** And they approached the man who was over Joseph's house and spoke to him at the doorway of the house.

**20** And they said, "Please, my lord — we came down the first time to buy food.

**21** And it came to pass when we came to the lodging place and we opened our sacks, and behold — each man's silver was in the mouth of his sack, our silver in its full weight. And we have brought it back in our hand.

**22** And other silver we have brought down in our hand to buy food. We do not know who put our silver in our sacks."

**23** And he said, "Peace be to you — do not be afraid. Your God and the God of your father has given you treasure in your sacks. Your silver came to me." And he brought Simeon out to them.

## Preparation for the Meal

**24** And the man brought the men into Joseph's house, and gave them water and they washed their feet, and he gave fodder to their donkeys.

**25** And they prepared the gift for the coming of Joseph at noon, for they had heard that they would eat bread there.

## Joseph's Reception: The Prostration and the Tears

**26** And Joseph came to the house, and they brought him the gift that was in their hand into the house, and they bowed down to him to the ground.

**27** And he asked them about their welfare and said, "Is your father well, the old man of whom you spoke? Is he still alive?"

**28** And they said, "Your servant our father is well — he is still alive." And they bowed down and prostrated themselves.

**29** And he lifted up his eyes and saw Benjamin his brother, the son of his mother, and said, "Is this your youngest brother of whom you spoke to me?" And he said, "God be gracious to you, my son."

**30** And Joseph hurried — for his compassion was stirred toward his brother, and he sought to weep. And he went into the inner room and wept there.

**31** And he washed his face and went out and controlled himself, and said, "Set out bread."

## The Meal: Separation and the Five-Fold Portion

**32** And they set out for him by himself and for them by themselves and for the Egyptians who ate with him by themselves, for the Egyptians could not eat bread with the Hebrews — for that is an abomination to Egypt.

**33** And they sat before him — the firstborn according to his birthright and the youngest according to his youth. And the men looked at one another in astonishment.

**34** And he took portions to them from before him, and Benjamin's portion was five times greater than any of their portions. And they drank and were drunk with him.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of two Leningrad Codex manuscript images and two Vaticanus manuscript images.

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 43 spans Folios 25v–26r across two manuscript images.

   **Folio 25v** (GEN_043_page_001.jpg): This is PDF page 49, the verso of Folio 25, photographed in inverted orientation in the WSRP image. The three-column layout is fully legible with Tiberian vocalization and cantillation throughout. This folio carries the end of Genesis 42 in the opening portion and then transitions into Genesis 43. The chapter division is visible with the parashah notation. The folio carries Genesis 43 from verse 1 through approximately verse 18. The opening verse — וְהָרָעָב כָּבֵד בָּאָרֶץ (v'haraav kaved ba-arets — "and the famine was severe in the land") — launches the chapter with one of its key structural motifs. Judah's two-part speech (vv. 3–5 and 8–10) and Jacob's blessing speech invoking El Shaddai (vv. 11–14) are all clearly legible with full vocalization. The arrival at Joseph's house (vv. 15–18) closes the folio's section of Genesis 43 content.

   **Folio 26r** (GEN_043_page_002.jpg): This is PDF page 50, the recto of Folio 26. The three-column layout is fully legible. This folio carries Genesis 43 from approximately verse 19 through verse 34 and continues into the opening verses of Genesis 44. The brothers' anxious explanation to the steward (vv. 19–22), the steward's remarkable speech invoking "your God and the God of your father" (v. 23), and Simeon's release (v. 23b) are clearly legible. The key scenes of the meal — the separate table arrangements (v. 32), the seating in birth order (v. 33), and Benjamin's five-fold portion (v. 34) — are all legible in the final section of the folio's Gen 43 content, after which Genesis 44 begins.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Two Vaticanus pages were read directly.

   **Page 43** (GEN_43_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, digi.vatlib.it page pa_0047): This page is shared with the end of Genesis 42 content. The chapter heading "CAP. XLVIII" is visible in the upper left margin, reflecting the Vaticanus chapter enumeration. The page carries Genesis 43 from its opening verses. The continuous uncial Greek text of Vaticanus is legible throughout this page with some surface wear concentrated on the outer columns. The three-column layout is clear; the right-hand column in particular carries the opening narrative of Genesis 43 with Judah's speech beginning to be legible.

   **Page 44** (GEN_43_vaticanus_page_002.jpg, digi.vatlib.it page pa_0048): This page carries the bulk of Genesis 43 and transitions into the beginning of Genesis 44. The chapter notation "CAP. XLIX." is visible in the left margin, marking what Vaticanus numbers as its chapter 49. The page is well preserved with clearly legible three-column uncial text. The meal scene, the separate Egyptian table, the seating in birth order, and Benjamin's five-fold portion are all recoverable from this page. The page also carries the opening of Genesis 44 (the cup-in-sack episode).

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "The famine was severe" — וְהָרָעָב כָּבֵד בָּאָרֶץ (v'haraav kaved ba-arets) (v. 1)**

The chapter opens by restating the condition that frames the entire Joseph narrative: the famine. The adjective כָּבֵד (kaved — "heavy, severe, weighty") is one of the most semantically rich words in Biblical Hebrew. Its root כ-ב-ד (k-v-d, the same root as kavod — "glory, weight, honor") communicates literal heaviness but carries the connotation of being oppressive, burdensome, overwhelming. A "heavy famine" is not merely a food shortage but an overwhelming, crushing scarcity — the same adjective used for the hardening of Pharaoh's heart (Ex 7:14), the weight of Jacob's grief (Gen 48:10), and the glory/weightiness of God (kabod).

The verse is grammatically set off from the narrative chain: it begins not with the wayyiqtol waw-consecutive of narrative but with the waw + perfect: וְהָרָעָב כָּבֵד (the famine was/remained severe). This construction has a durative or circumstantial force — the famine is the ongoing background condition against which the drama plays out. It was already severe at the end of chapter 42 and remains so. The brief syntactic pause — a one-clause verse in stark simplicity — gives the chapter a weighty opening: everything that follows happens under this condition of pressing need.

The LXX renders the verse with its characteristic expansion: ὁ δὲ λιμὸς ἐνίσχυσεν ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς (ho de limos enischysen epi tes ges — "and the famine grew strong upon the land"). The Greek verb ἐνίσχυσεν ("grew strong, prevailed, intensified") is more dynamic than the MT's stative adjective; the LXX portrays the famine as actively pressing upon the land rather than simply remaining heavy. The formal translation follows the MT's stative formulation: "the famine was severe in the land."

**2. "The man solemnly warned us" — הָעֵד הֵעִד בָּנוּ הָאִישׁ (ha-ed he'id banu ha-ish) (v. 3)**

Judah's speech opens with a construction that gives the translation difficulty: the Hiphil infinitive absolute הָעֵד followed by the Hiphil perfect הֵעִד — the famous Hebrew construction of verb + infinitive absolute of the same root for emphatic intensification: "testifying he testified to us," i.e., "he solemnly warned us, he emphatically testified to us." The root ע-ו-ד (a-v-d or a-w-d — "to testify, to bear witness, to warn solemnly") in the Hiphil (he'id) means to give solemn testimony, to warn with the force of a formal declaration.

The construction is a legal idiom: the Hiphil of 'ud (to warn/testify) with its infinitive absolute is the same form used in covenant warnings — the formal, binding, repeated declaration that carries legal and covenantal weight. Joseph's declaration about Benjamin was not a casual remark but a solemn, repeated insistence: "he earnestly declared to us" or "he solemnly testified to us." The formal translation renders "solemnly warned," which captures both the legal register and the force of the infinitive absolute without becoming awkward in English.

The significance is that Judah is characterizing Joseph's demand as a formal, binding condition — one that cannot be circumvented or ignored. This is Judah setting up his argument to his father: this is not an arbitrary preference of the Egyptian official but a solemn, declared condition that has the force of law.

The LXX renders the phrase as ἐπιμαρτυρόμενος ἐμαρτύρατο ἡμῖν ὁ ἄνθρωπος (epimartyromenos emartryrato hemin ho anthropos — "the man testified to us with solemn testimony"). The LXX uses the same intensification device in Greek — an emphatic participle followed by the finite verb — demonstrating that the Hellenistic translators understood and preserved the Hebrew infinitive absolute construction.

**3. "I myself will be surety for him" — אָנֹכִי אֶעֶרְבֶנּוּ (anokhi e'ervennu) (v. 9)**

Judah's pledge in verse 9 is one of the most consequential sentences in the Joseph narrative. The first word — אָנֹכִי (anokhi — the emphatic personal pronoun "I myself") — is emphatic by its very use: Hebrew normally does not require an explicit subject pronoun with a conjugated verb (the verb form already encodes person, number, and gender). When the pronoun is added, it is for emphasis: "I — I myself — I alone will be surety." The pronoun anokhi (as opposed to the shorter ani) is the more archaic and emphatic form, used in high moments of first-person declaration throughout the Torah.

The verb אֶעֶרְבֶנּוּ (e'ervennu) is the Qal imperfect of ע-ר-ב (a-r-v — "to give surety, to stand as guarantee, to pledge oneself as a pledge for another"). The root עָרֹב (arov — "pledge, surety") is the same root from which the noun עֵרֵב (erev — "guarantor, surety") is derived, and which appears in the financial and legal sense of a guarantor who stands between a creditor and a debtor, taking personal liability for the debt. Judah is offering himself as a legal guarantor: if Benjamin is not returned, Judah will bear the liability personally, before his father.

The verb with the third-person masculine singular suffix: e'ervennu — "I will be surety for him" — places Benjamin as the object of Judah's pledge. The surety relationship is constituted in this one word: Judah becomes responsible for Benjamin's safe return.

The narrative significance is immense. Judah was the brother who suggested selling Joseph (37:26–27): "What profit is it if we kill our brother and conceal his blood? Come, let us sell him." The profit-calculation that moved Judah in chapter 37 is now reversed: in chapter 43 Judah offers himself as surety for his brother, with no profit calculation, only liability. The trajectory from 37:26 to 43:9 is the arc of Judah's character transformation — the culmination of which comes in 44:33 when he offers himself as a slave in Benjamin's place. Here at verse 9 is the midpoint of that arc: the surety pledge.

The LXX renders anokhi e'ervennu as ἐγὼ δέξομαι αὐτόν (ego dexomai auton — "I will receive/accept him for myself" or "I take charge of him") — a somewhat different formulation: not "I will be surety" in the legal-financial sense but "I will take him in charge, I will accept responsibility for him." The Greek ἐγώ (ego) matches the emphatic anokhi; the verb δέξομαι ("I will take/accept") renders the surety relationship in terms of taking charge rather than the legal pledge-language of the MT. The formal translation follows MT: "I myself will be surety for him."

**4. "El Shaddai give you compassion" — וְאֵל שַׁדַּי יִתֵּן לָכֶם רַחֲמִים (v'el Shaddai yitten lakhem rachamim) (v. 14)**

Jacob's blessing speech in verse 14 reaches its climax with the invocation of El Shaddai — the divine name that has appeared at key covenant moments throughout Genesis (17:1, when God makes the covenant with Abraham; 28:3, Isaac's blessing of Jacob; 35:11, God's reassurance at Bethel). The name El Shaddai appears at precisely those moments when the promise appears most under threat and when the patriarch must commit his family's survival to the care of a God whose ways exceed human calculation.

**El Shaddai** is preserved untranslated per the established project convention. The etymology is uncertain: שַׁדַּי (Shaddai) has been variously connected to the Akkadian shadu ("mountain"), to the Hebrew root שׁ-ד-ד (sh-d-d — "to be powerful, to overpower"), and to the Hebrew שַׁד (shad — "breast"), yielding possible renderings of "God of the mountains," "God Almighty," "God who overpowers," or "God who nourishes/sustains." The LXX consistently renders Shaddai as ὁ θεός μου (my God) or with ἱκανός (hikanos — "the Sufficient One, the All-Sufficient"), from which later Latin tradition developed "Omnipotens" (Almighty). The etymological uncertainty and the specific covenantal character of the name counsel preservation rather than translation.

The word רַחֲמִים (rachamim — "compassion, mercy, tender mercies") is the plural of רֶחֶם (rechem — "womb"), and rachamim in the plural carries the force of "deep, visceral compassion" — the kind of compassion a mother has for the child of her womb, expanded to a theological concept of God's tender mercy toward his people. Jacob prays that El Shaddai will give them rachamim — compassion, womb-love — "before the man." The word choice is deeply significant: Jacob is not merely asking for favorable treatment but for a quality of merciful feeling to arise in Joseph's heart toward the brothers.

The irony is dense: Jacob is praying to God for compassion from "the man" in Egypt — and "the man" is Joseph, his own son, who has been harboring precisely this compassion for years but has been controlling it. The rachamim Jacob prays for is already present in Joseph; what Joseph is doing is managing it, channeling it toward the full fulfillment of the test. El Shaddai's answer to Jacob's prayer is already prepared — it will appear in verse 30: Joseph's compassion is stirred (חָמְרוּ רַחֲמָיו, chamru rachamav — the exact same word family) and he weeps privately.

Jacob closes with the resigned acceptance — "if I am bereaved, I am bereaved" (וַאֲנִי כַּאֲשֶׁר שָׁכֹלְתִּי שָׁכָלְתִּי, va-ani ka'asher shakhilti shakhilti) — using again the Piel of שׁ-כ-ל (sh-k-l) from 42:36, the bereavement verb. This is the same resigned acceptance we saw in Esther 4:16 ("If I perish, I perish"). It is not despair but a release of the outcome to God: I will do what I must; the rest is not mine to control.

The LXX renders Jacob's resignation as κἀμοὶ καθὰ ἠτέκνωμαι ἠτέκνωμαι (kamoi katha eteknomen eteknomen — "and as for me, as I have been bereaved, I have been bereaved") — using the same Greek verb as in 42:36 and maintaining the same double-verb construction. The LXX preserves the resigned double expression faithfully.

**5. "Slaughter an animal and prepare it" — טְבֹחַ טֶבַח (tevoach tevach) (v. 16)**

When Joseph sees Benjamin, his first action is a command to the steward: bring the men to the house and "slaughter an animal and prepare it" (טְבֹחַ טֶבַח — tevoach tevach — "slaughter a slaughter"). Again the infinitive absolute construction is used for emphasis and vividness: not merely "kill an animal" but the directive with its doubled root — a formal, emphatic command for the festive preparation of a meal.

The word טֶבַח (tevach — "slaughtered animal, slaughter, butcher-work") is distinct from the more common vocabulary of sacrifice: this is domestic animal slaughter for a feast, not a religious offering. The root ט-ב-ח (t-v-ch — "to slaughter, to butcher") is the root of the title טַבָּח (tabbah — "butcher, cook, executioner"), and the same root from which Potiphar's title in Gen 37:36 and 39:1 (sar ha-tabbahim — "captain of the guard/executioner-chief") is derived. Here it is the command for feast preparation.

The emphasis — both the infinitive absolute and the doubled root — signals that this is a formal, exceptional feast, not an ordinary meal. Joseph is planning a royal entertainment for his brothers. The meal that will follow (vv. 31–34) will be organized with precise protocol: separate tables, precise seating by birth order, the five-fold portion for Benjamin. The command here is the preparation for that carefully orchestrated event.

The LXX renders tevoach tevach as σφάξον θύματα (sphaxon thymata — "slaughter victims/sacrificial animals") — using both a verb and a noun, somewhat expanding the MT's doubled root. The LXX's θύματα ("sacrificial animals, victims") carries a slightly more religious register than the MT's secular domestic slaughter terminology.

**6. "Slipping the money into our sacks" — לְהִתְגֹּלֵל עָלֵינוּ (l'hitgolel aleinu) (v. 18)**

The brothers' fear upon being brought to Joseph's house is expressed in verse 18 with a striking verb: לְהִתְגֹּלֵל עָלֵינוּ (l'hitgolel aleinu — "to roll against us, to roll over upon us"). The Hitpoel/Hithpael of ג-ל-ל (g-l-l — "to roll") in this form has the sense of "to roll oneself upon someone," implying the sudden, overwhelming falling-upon of an adversary: to pounce upon, to attack by overwhelming force. It is a vivid, physical metaphor for the feeling of being ambushed — the fear that the Egyptians will simply crash down upon them.

The brothers' fear reveals their guilt: they immediately assume the worst. The returned silver, which was Joseph's gift, they interpret as a trap — a pretext to seize them. Their guilty conscience makes the gracious act look threatening. This is a subtle psychological detail: men who have done wrong are quick to expect punishment even in innocent situations.

The formal translation renders "in order to roll over us and fall upon us" — preserving the physical-metaphor force of the Hitpoel while making it intelligible in English. Some translations render simply "to attack us" (which loses the rolling/overwhelming imagery) or "to overwhelm us" (which is acceptable but more abstract).

**7. "Peace be to you — do not be afraid" — שָׁלוֹם לָכֶם אַל תִּירָאוּ (shalom lakhem al tira'u) (v. 23)**

The steward's response to the brothers' anxious explanation is one of the most striking speeches of the chapter. He does not merely reassure them about the money — he invokes theology: "Your God and the God of your father has given you treasure in your sacks." The steward is an Egyptian; he is speaking of Israel's God in terms that place the returned money within the framework of divine gift, not Egyptian bookkeeping.

The title שָׁלוֹם (shalom) — "peace, wholeness, well-being" — as an opening word to the brothers is the declaration of the chapter's thematic counterpoint: where the brothers feared "rolling over" (v. 18), the steward gives שָׁלוֹם. Where they expected accusation and seizure, they receive the Torah's great comprehensive word for right relationship and flourishing.

The steward's statement that "your silver came to me" (וְכַסְפְּכֶם בָּא אֵלָי, v'khaspekhem ba elai) is an aside that the narrative does not develop: the steward is explaining that the money was properly received — implying that Joseph arranged for both the silver to go in the sacks and for the steward to be paid from another source, or that this is a fiction designed to prevent the brothers' legal anxiety. Either way, the steward is Joseph's instrument of reassurance.

The theological dimension of the steward's speech — "Your God and the God of your father has given you treasure" (אֱלֹהֵיכֶם וֵאלֹהֵי אֲבִיכֶם נָתַן לָכֶם מַטְמוֹן בְּאַמְתְּחֹתֵיכֶם, Eloheikhem vElohei avikhem natan lakhem matmon b'amtechoteikhem) — is remarkable. An Egyptian household steward is articulating the providence of Israel's God. Whether the steward is a convert, an Israelite in Joseph's service, or is simply repeating Joseph's own interpretation of events (which Joseph has communicated to him), the effect is the same: the first theological interpretation of the returned money comes not from Jacob or the brothers but from an Egyptian. The "treasure" (מַטְמוֹן — matmon) the steward identifies is God's hidden gift.

**8. "His compassion was stirred toward his brother" — כִּי נִכְמְרוּ רַחֲמָיו אֶל אָחִיו (ki nikhm'ru rachamav el achiv) (v. 30)**

The two-part emotional description of verse 30 is among the most psychologically precise texts in Genesis. The first element: כִּי נִכְמְרוּ רַחֲמָיו אֶל אָחִיו (ki nikhm'ru rachamav el achiv — "for his compassion was stirred/grew warm toward his brother"). The verb נִכְמְרוּ (nikhm'ru) is from the root כ-מ-ר (k-m-r — "to become warm, to glow with heat, to be stirred with warmth"), used here in the Niphal to describe the internal kindling of emotional warmth. The word rachamim (compassion — from the same root as rechem, "womb") now "grows hot" or "is stirred" in Joseph's body at the sight of Benjamin.

This is the same word family Jacob invoked in verse 14 when he prayed that El Shaddai would give them rachamim before "the man." The prayer of verse 14 is answered in verse 30: Joseph's rachamim stirs toward his own brother. The inclusion of the prayer and its fulfillment within the same chapter is deliberate: the narrator is tracking the correspondence between Jacob's prayer and God's answer.

The second element: וַיְבַקֵּשׁ לִבְכּוֹת (vayevakkesh livkot — "and he sought to weep"). The Piel of בּ-ק-שׁ (b-q-sh — "to seek, to look for, to want") with the infinitive construct: "he sought weeping" — i.e., he felt the urge to weep coming upon him and had to go find a place to do so privately. The construction is peculiarly exact: he does not weep on the spot; he has to manage the emotion. He seeks a weeping place.

This is Joseph's second weeping scene (cf. 42:24). At 42:24 he turned away and wept after hearing the brothers' confession. Here the trigger is seeing Benjamin — his full brother, the son of his mother Rachel. The sight of Benjamin re-activates all of Joseph's loss and longing in a single moment. But he cannot yet weep publicly, cannot yet reveal himself. The test is still incomplete. So he manages: he seeks a private place, weeps, washes his face (v. 31: יִרְחַץ פָּנָיו — yirchats panav), returns, controls himself (וַיִּתְאַפַּק — vayyit'appak — "and he controlled/restrained himself"), and commands the meal.

The verb וַיִּתְאַפַּק (vayyit'appak — "and he controlled himself, restrained himself") is from א-פ-ק (a-p-q — "to restrain, to hold back, to exercise self-control"), here in the Hithpael reflexive: "he held himself in, he exercised self-restraint upon himself." It is the precise antonym of the moment that will come in 45:1: וְלֹא יָכֹל יוֹסֵף לְהִתְאַפֵּק (v'lo yakol Yosef l'hit'appek — "and Joseph could not restrain himself"). Here in 43:31 he can still restrain himself; in 45:1 the restraint breaks. The distance between "could restrain" and "could not restrain" is the distance between Genesis 43 and Genesis 45.

**9. "That is an abomination to Egypt" — כִּי תוֹעֵבָה הִוא לְמִצְרַיִם (ki to'evah hi l'Mitsrayim) (v. 32)**

The statement that explains the separate dining arrangements is theologically and culturally loaded: כִּי תוֹעֵבָה הִוא לְמִצְרַיִם (ki to'evah hi l'Mitsrayim — "for that is an abomination to Egypt"). The word תּוֹעֵבָה (to'evah — "abomination, detestable thing") is one of the strongest negative evaluative terms in the Hebrew Bible. It is the word used in Leviticus and Deuteronomy for the worst violations of the covenant: idolatry (Deut 7:25), sexual violations (Lev 18:22–30), dishonest weights (Deut 25:16), and various other things that evoke radical revulsion, incompatibility, the sense that two things cannot coexist in the same space.

Here the narrator applies the term ironically: it is the Egyptians who have the to'evah, and the to'evah is eating with Hebrews. The Egyptians view mixing food with foreigners — specifically Hebrews — as a form of contamination or ritual incompatibility. The social reality this reflects accords with what Egyptian sources tell us about the social stratification of the New Kingdom and the symbolic importance of table-fellowship. Hebrews (ibrim — likely related to the Habiru/Apiru of Egyptian documents, a social class of migrants and marginal workers) were a distinct and lower-status group in Egyptian society; sharing their meal would be a form of ritual condescension or contamination.

The irony the narrator maintains is profound: the man eating at the separate table of the Egyptian ruler, alone (וְלוֹ לְבַדּוֹ — v'lo levado — "and for him by himself," i.e., Joseph alone), is himself a Hebrew. Joseph sits alone because he is the Egyptian viceroy and cannot eat with his subjects; but he is a Hebrew, and the very brothers whose food he cannot share are his own flesh. The three tiers of the meal — Joseph alone, the brothers, the Egyptians — are a social map of the paradox of Joseph's situation: he is the most powerful Egyptian in the room and simultaneously the most Hebrew.

The LXX renders to'evah as βδέλυγμα (bdelygma — "abomination, abhorrent thing"), the standard LXX equivalent for to'evah. The word βδέλυγμα appears in the NT in the phrase "abomination of desolation" (Matt 24:15; Mark 13:14; Dan 9:27 LXX), preserving the radical incompatibility/revulsion connotation.

**10. "The firstborn according to his birthright and the youngest according to his youth" — הַבְּכֹר כִּבְכֹרָתוֹ וְהַצָּעִיר כִּצְעִרָתוֹ (ha-bekhor kiv'korato v'ha-tsa'ir k'tse'irato) (v. 33)**

The seating of the brothers in precise birth order by Joseph's instruction is one of the chapter's most dramatically effective moments. The brothers are seated by an Egyptian official from oldest to youngest — and they look at each other in astonishment (וַיִּתְמְהוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים אִישׁ אֶל רֵעֵהוּ, vayyitm'hu ha-anashim ish el re'ehu — "and the men were astonished, each man toward his neighbor"). They cannot explain how this Egyptian could know their birth order.

The phrasing is elegantly parallel: הַבְּכֹר כִּבְכֹרָתוֹ (ha-bekhor kiv'korato — "the firstborn according to his birthright/first-born-status") and וְהַצָּעִיר כִּצְעִרָתוֹ (v'ha-tsa'ir k'tse'irato — "and the youngest according to his youth/youngest-status"). The parallelism locks the two endpoints in place: the eldest at the top of the order, the youngest at the bottom, and implicitly all others in between. The word בְּכֹרָה (b'koratho — "birthright, first-born status") is the same word involved in Esau's sale of his birthright to Jacob in Genesis 25:31–34. The narrator's use of b'korat/birthright in the seating scene is a quiet irony: the Joseph narrative has been shaped by the reversal of birthright norms (Joseph the younger over the older; Benjamin the youngest receives the largest portion); now the older order is laid out before Joseph, who sees and controls it all.

The brothers' astonishment (וַיִּתְמְהוּ — vayyitm'hu — from ת-מ-ה, t-m-h — "to be astonished, dumbfounded") is also their near-recognition moment that the narrator exploits: they cannot figure out how he knows their order. The explanation, which they do not yet have, will come in 45:3 when Joseph reveals himself. The impossible knowledge of the seating is one of the clues being accumulated for that revelation.

**11. "Benjamin's portion was five times greater" — וַיֶּרֶב מַשְׂאַת בִּנְיָמִן מִמַּשְׂאֹת כֻּלָּם חָמֵשׁ יָדוֹת (vayyerev mas'at Binyamin mimmas'ot kullam chamesh yadot) (v. 34)**

The five-fold portion for Benjamin is the chapter's climactic gesture and one of the narrative's most discussed details. The word מַשְׂאַת (mas'at — "portion, lifting-up, gift, tribute") is from נ-שׂ-א (n-s'-a — "to lift, to bear, to carry"), the standard term for a portion taken and lifted from a larger supply: the host's portion served to a guest from his own table. In this sense, mas'at is both the physical food and the honor of the gift.

The phrase חָמֵשׁ יָדוֹת (chamesh yadot — "five hands/portions") is striking. The word יָדוֹת (yadot) is the plural of יָד (yad — "hand"), and in the phrase chamesh yadot it means "five measures" or "five portions" — five times the standard. The word yad-as-measure appears elsewhere (cf. 2 Kings 11:7 — "two hands/thirds of you," i.e., two thirds). The five-fold excess is not merely generous; it is a test.

The careful reader of the Joseph narrative will recognize the typological resonance: this chapter is a mirror of the original act that started the whole crisis. In Genesis 37:3, Jacob gave Joseph an exceptional garment — a token of favoritism. In 43:34, Joseph gives Benjamin an exceptional portion — five times that of the others. Joseph is replicating the dynamics of favoritism that drove his own suffering to see how the brothers respond. Will they, as before (37:4), become jealous and bitter at the favored younger brother? Will they do to Benjamin what they did to Joseph?

The answer is given in the final clause: "and they drank and were drunk with him" (וַיִּשְׁתּוּ וַיִּשְׁכְּרוּ עִמּוֹ, vayyishtu vayyishkkeru immo). The brothers feast together. No jealousy is recorded. The verb וַיִּשְׁכְּרוּ (vayyishk'ru — "and they were drunk, they drank freely") is from שׁ-כ-ר (sh-k-r — "to be/become drunk, to drink to satiety/fullness"), used here as an indication of complete, uninhibited fellowship at the table. They are "merry" with Benjamin, not resentful. The test is being passed — the brothers have not manifested the sibling jealousy of old. The five-fold gift finds its answer in companionable drinking.

The LXX renders chamesh yadot as πενταπλασίως (pentaplasios — "fivefold, five times as much") — collapsing the idiomatic "five hands" into a single Greek adverb but preserving the ratio exactly. The formal translation follows MT: "five times greater."

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 43

Pages 43–44 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. Page 43 (digi.vatlib.it pa_0047) carries the chapter from its opening through approximately the middle section; page 44 (digi.vatlib.it pa_0048) carries the remainder of the chapter and into Genesis 44. Page 44 shows "CAP. XLIX." in the left margin, reflecting the Vaticanus chapter enumeration. Key variants and equivalences observed:

| Verse (MT) | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 43:1 | וְהָרָעָב כָּבֵד בָּאָרֶץ ("and the famine was severe in the land") | ὁ δὲ λιμὸς ἐνίσχυσεν ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς ("and the famine grew strong upon the land") | MT followed | The LXX uses a dynamic verb (ἐνίσχυσεν — "grew strong, intensified") where the MT has a stative adjective (kaved — "heavy, severe"). The LXX portrays the famine as actively pressing; the MT presents it as a settled, weighty condition. The formal translation follows the MT's stative construction. |
| 43:3 | הָעֵד הֵעִד בָּנוּ ("he solemnly warned us") | ἐπιμαρτυρόμενος ἐμαρτύρατο ἡμῖν ("testifying he testified to us") | Equivalent | Both MT and LXX use the doubled-root construction (infinitive absolute + finite verb in Hebrew; participial intensification + finite verb in Greek) to render the emphatic warning. The LXX preserves the structural device of intensification rather than simply rendering the meaning. |
| 43:9 | אָנֹכִי אֶעֶרְבֶנּוּ ("I myself will be surety for him") | ἐγὼ δέξομαι αὐτόν ("I will take him in charge") | MT followed | The MT's legal surety language (a-r-v — "to pledge, to stand surety") is rendered by the LXX with δέξομαι ("I will accept/receive/take responsibility for") — a more general acceptance of responsibility. The formal translation follows the MT's specific surety terminology: "I myself will be surety for him." |
| 43:11 | נְכֹאת וּדְבַשׁ נְכֹאת לֹט בְּטָנִים וּשְׁקֵדִים ("balm and honey, tragacanth and laudanum, pistachios and almonds") | θυμίαμα καὶ μέλι καὶ θυμίαμα καὶ στακτὴν καὶ τερέβινθον καὶ κάρυα ("incense and honey and incense and stacte and terebinth and nuts") | MT followed | The gift list is rendered differently in the LXX. The MT has: נְכֹאת (n'kho'at — likely tragacanth gum or aromatic resin), דְּבַשׁ (devash — honey), לֹט (lot — laudanum/labdanum, a resin), בְּטָנִים (b'tanim — pistachios), שְׁקֵדִים (sh'kedim — almonds). The LXX renders θυμίαμα twice (for the two resin-terms), and τερέβινθον (terebinth) for the pistachios. The formal translation follows the MT's specific botanical terms with approximate renderings. |
| 43:14 | וְאֵל שַׁדַּי ("and El Shaddai") | ὁ δὲ θεός μου ("and my God") | MT followed | The divine name El Shaddai is preserved untranslated per established convention. The LXX renders Shaddai with a possessive personal reference (my God), losing the specific covenantal name. The formal translation preserves El Shaddai. |
| 43:14 | כַּאֲשֶׁר שָׁכֹלְתִּי שָׁכָלְתִּי ("if I am bereaved, I am bereaved") | κἀμοὶ καθὰ ἠτέκνωμαι ἠτέκνωμαι ("and as for me, as I have been bereaved, I have been bereaved") | Equivalent | Both MT and LXX preserve the doubled-verb resignation formula. The LXX uses ἀτεκνόω (the same word used for Jacob's bereavement in 42:36) and maintains the resigned double expression. |
| 43:16 | טְבֹחַ טֶבַח ("slaughter a slaughter / slaughter an animal") | σφάξον θύματα ("slaughter sacrificial victims") | MT followed | The LXX's θύματα ("sacrificial victims, animals offered") carries a slightly more religious register than the MT's secular domestic slaughter (tevach — butcher-work). The formal translation renders "slaughter an animal and prepare it" following the domestic sense of the MT. |
| 43:23 | אֱלֹהֵיכֶם וֵאלֹהֵי אֲבִיכֶם ("your God and the God of your father") | ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν καὶ ὁ θεὸς τοῦ πατρὸς ὑμῶν ("your God and the God of your father") | Equivalent | The steward's theological identification of God is rendered equivalently in both MT and LXX. |
| 43:29 | יִשְׂרָאֵל ("Israel") | In this context the MT has יִשְׂרָאֵל for Jacob throughout; LXX has Ισραηλ | Equivalent | The names Jacob/Israel are used in the MT of chapter 43 with specific pattern: "Israel" (vv. 6, 8, 11) for the patriarch's formal/theological role as the nation's father; "Jacob" only appears in vv. 2. The LXX follows the same pattern. |
| 43:29 | חָנְּךָ אֱלֹהִים בְּנִי ("God be gracious to you, my son") | ὁ θεὸς ἐλεήσαί σε τέκνον ("may God have mercy on you, child") | Near-equivalent | The MT's root ח-נ-ן (ch-n-n — "to be gracious, to show favor, to grant grace") is rendered by the LXX with ἐλεέω (eleeō — "to have mercy, to show compassion"). The two words overlap but are not identical: חנינה (grace, favor) emphasizes undeserved gift from a superior; ἔλεος (mercy/compassion) emphasizes the quality of emotional responsiveness to need. Both carry the sense of gracious gift; the formal translation follows the MT's grace-language: "God be gracious to you, my son." |
| 43:30 | נִכְמְרוּ רַחֲמָיו ("his compassion was stirred") | ἐταράχθη τὰ ἔντερα αὐτοῦ ("his bowels/innards were disturbed") | MT followed | This is a significant LXX variant. The MT: nikhm'ru rachamav (from k-m-r — "to glow with warmth, to be stirred with compassion" + rachamim — "womb-compassion"). The LXX: etarachthe ta entera autou ("his bowels were troubled/disturbed") — using the Greek anatomical idiom for deep emotional disturbance (the bowels as the seat of emotion in Greek physiology, corresponding to the Hebrew rechem/rachamim as the womb/belly-seat of compassion). Both idioms reach for the visceral location of emotion, but the MT's rachamim carries the specific theological term for God's compassion (rachamim — the same word Jacob prayed for in v. 14), which the LXX rendering loses. The formal translation follows the MT: "his compassion was stirred." |
| 43:32 | כִּי תוֹעֵבָה הִוא לְמִצְרַיִם ("for that is an abomination to Egypt") | βδέλυγμά ἐστιν τοῖς Αἰγυπτίοις ("for it is an abomination to the Egyptians") | Equivalent | MT to'evah = LXX bdelygma. Both are the strongest available term for radical ritual incompatibility/revulsion. The formal translation renders "abomination." |
| 43:34 | חָמֵשׁ יָדוֹת ("five hands/portions") | πενταπλασίως ("fivefold") | MT followed | The MT's idiomatic "five hands" (chamesh yadot — five measures) is rendered by the LXX with a single adverb for the ratio. The formal translation renders "five times greater," following the MT's ratio while making the meaning explicit in English. |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Genesis 43**

Genesis 43 is the pivot chapter of the entire Joseph narrative. Coming between the crisis of Genesis 42 (first Egypt visit, first test, silver discovered, Benjamin withheld) and the climax of Genesis 44–45 (the cup in the sack, Judah's speech, the self-revelation), Genesis 43 functions as the hinge on which everything turns. The structure:

- **Vv. 1–2**: The situation — famine severe, grain exhausted; Jacob's pragmatic prompt to return
- **Vv. 3–5**: Judah's first speech — the condition reiterated; no Benjamin, no trip
- **Vv. 6–7**: Jacob's complaint; brothers' defense
- **Vv. 8–10**: Judah's second speech — the surety pledge; the delay argument
- **Vv. 11–14**: Jacob's gift list and El Shaddai blessing — the patriarch's submission
- **V. 15**: Departure — men, gifts, double silver, Benjamin
- **Vv. 16–25**: Arrival and anxiety — Joseph sees Benjamin; steward brings them in; brothers fear; steward reassures; Simeon freed; preparation
- **Vv. 26–29**: Gifts presented; prostration; Joseph asks about father; Joseph sees Benjamin; blesses him
- **V. 30**: Joseph's private weeping — the emotional pivot
- **Vv. 31–34**: The meal — three-table arrangement; seating by birth order; Benjamin's five-fold portion; feasting

The chapter's architecture moves from Jacob's paralysis (v. 2: "go buy us food") through the elaborate negotiation that breaks the paralysis (vv. 3–14) to the pivotal meeting (vv. 26–34). The axis of the chapter is verse 30 — Joseph's weeping — which is the emotional center of the narrative. Everything before verse 30 builds toward it; everything after proceeds from it.

**Judah's Emergence as Leader**

A major structural development of Genesis 43 is the transfer of leadership speech from Reuben (chapter 42) to Judah (chapter 43). In 42:37 it was Reuben who offered a surety for Benjamin — "put my two sons to death if I do not bring him back." Jacob refused. In 43:3–5 and 43:8–10 it is Judah who speaks, and his speech succeeds where Reuben's failed.

The contrast between the two offers is instructive. Reuben's offer (42:37) was transactional and slightly absurd: "put my two sons to death" — offering Jacob his grandchildren as hostages. The offer misreads Jacob's psychology: Jacob is trying to protect his children, not acquire more pawns. Judah's offer (43:9) is personal and unqualified: "I myself will be surety for him — from my hand you shall require him. If I do not bring him back, I will have sinned against you all the days of my life." No hostages, no collateral — only Judah's personal guarantee, stated with the emphatic anokhi ("I myself"). And crucially: "all the days of my life" — this is a lifetime pledge, not a temporary arrangement.

Jacob accepts Judah's offer. Why? Because Judah offers what Jacob needs: not a contingency plan but a personal commitment that places Judah's own standing before Jacob at permanent risk. The anokhi e'ervennu of verse 9 is the turning point of Judah's character development, the midpoint of the transformation that began in chapter 38 (Judah's recognition — hakker-na — and confession before Tamar) and that will reach its climax in 44:33 ("let your servant remain instead of the boy as a slave to my lord"). Genesis 43:9 is the first act of self-substitution.

**The Double Prostration**

Verse 26 records that the brothers brought Joseph the gifts and "bowed down to him to the ground" (וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ לוֹ אָרְצָה, vayyishtachavu lo artsah). Verse 28 records that "they bowed down and prostrated themselves" (וַיִּקְּדוּ וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ, vayyikk'du vayyishtachavu). The chapter contains a double prostration — two bowing gestures in close succession. The first (v. 26) is the formal court greeting upon arriving with the gifts; the second (v. 28) is the response to Joseph's question about their father: when they report that their father "is still alive" (עוֹד חַי, od chai), they bow again.

Together with the bowing of chapter 42:6, this chapter now has three prostration events. The dream of the eleven sheaves bowing is approaching completion: ten brothers bowed in chapter 42; all eleven bow in Genesis 43 when Benjamin is included. The prostration scenes are the narrator's careful tracking of the dream's fulfillment.

**Jacob's Speech as Theological Turning Point**

Jacob's speech in verses 11–14 marks his transition from resistant patriarch to surrendered one. In 42:36–38 he refused to send Benjamin and declared his refusal in terms of grief and accumulated loss. In 43:11–14 he sends Benjamin — but he does not do so without theological framing. The gifts he lists (balm, honey, tragacanth, laudanum, pistachios, almonds) recall the trading caravan that carried Joseph to Egypt (37:25: "a caravan of Ishmaelites coming from Gilead, with their camels bearing tragacanth, balm, and laudanum"). The same aromatic resins and trade goods that carried Joseph away are now carried toward Egypt by his brothers.

The El Shaddai invocation is the theological center of the speech. Jacob calls on the name of God that has appeared at moments of covenant vulnerability — the same name God used when appearing to Abraham at the covenant of circumcision (17:1), the same name Isaac used when blessing Jacob at Paddan Aram (28:3), the same name God used when confirming the covenant with Jacob at Bethel (35:11). El Shaddai is the name of God as the one who makes covenant promises under impossible conditions and fulfills them. Jacob's invocation is both prayer and theological statement: I am releasing my sons — and my son — into the care of the God who keeps covenant promises even when human circumstances appear to foreclose them.

**The Five-Fold Portion and the Test of the Brothers**

Benjamin's five-fold portion (v. 34) is not merely narrative color. It is a deliberate repetition of the dynamic of chapter 37: the father's favoritism toward the younger-born son, the gift that marks a son as specially loved. In 37:3, Jacob gave Joseph a distinctive garment, and "his brothers saw that their father loved him more than any of his brothers." Their response: "they hated him and could not speak peaceably to him" (37:4).

Joseph, in 43:34, gives Benjamin five times the portion of any of the other brothers. He is replicating the test: will the ten brothers resent the favor shown to the eleventh? The narrative answer is given in the final words of the chapter: "and they drank and were drunk with him." There is no note of jealousy, no bitter word, no plot forming. The brothers feast with Benjamin. The hatred of chapter 37 has not been reprised.

This is the positive evidence that the brothers are changing. But the definitive proof will come in the cup-in-the-sack episode of chapter 44, where they will be given the opportunity to abandon Benjamin (just as they abandoned Joseph) — and Judah will refuse, offering himself as a slave in Benjamin's place. Genesis 43:34 is the quiet prelude to that climax: the brothers drink with Benjamin; in Genesis 44 they will be willing to die with him.

**The Meal as Eschatological Image**

The meal of Genesis 43 — three separate tables, brothers seated in order, the host weeping privately and then composing himself, the outsider given the greatest portion — has been read in the tradition as a foretaste of the great eschatological feast of reconciliation. The Messianic banquet motifs of Isaiah 25:6 (a feast on this mountain for all peoples), Luke 14:15 ("blessed is everyone who will eat bread in the kingdom of God"), and Revelation 19:9 ("blessed are those who are invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb") share with Genesis 43 the pattern of: unexpected host, surprising guest-list, separation overcome by the host's generosity, weeping and joy in proximity.

Joseph is the host who knows more than his guests know. He seats them in the order they cannot explain. He gives the outsider — Benjamin, the one who was not part of the original betrayal, the one who carries the most loaded history with Joseph — the greatest portion. He weeps in private and serves in composure. The meal is not yet the full reconciliation — that comes in chapter 45 — but it is its foretaste: the table is set, the portions are generous, the brothers drink and are merry. Under the surface, the long process of reconciliation is finding its form.
